---
# BLIND-REVIEW SWAPS (currently in CAMERA-READY mode).
# To switch to ARR blind mode:
#   • set _quarto.yml to `anonymous: true, review: true`
#   • replace the title below with the bare "Benchmarking …" form
#   • comment out the `author:` and `author-tex:` blocks
#   • apply the per-paragraph BLIND-REVIEW SWAP comments throughout the body
#   • comment out the Acknowledgments section
#   • comment out Appendix O (Responsible NLP Checklist)
#
# Blind title:
#   title: "Benchmarking LLM Systems on Subsumption-Based Legal Reasoning in German Law"
title: "BenGER: Benchmarking LLM Systems on Subsumption-Based Legal Reasoning in German Law"
jupyter: benger-dataset-arr
author:
  - name: Sebastian Nagl
    affiliation: Technical University of Munich (TUM)
    email: sebastian.nagl@tum.de
    attributes:
      corresponding: true
    note: "Core contributor"
  - name: Ann-Kristin Mayrhofer
    affiliation: Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich (LMU)
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Martin Heidebach
    affiliation: Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich (LMU)
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Aleyna Koçak
    affiliation: University of Konstanz
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Anne Zettelmeier
    affiliation: University of Saarbrücken
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Elly Breu
    affiliation: Technical University of Munich (TUM)
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Angelina Greiner
    affiliation: Technical University of Munich (TUM)
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Sofija Milijas
    affiliation: Technical University of Munich (TUM)
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Matthias Grabmair
    affiliation: Technical University of Munich (TUM)
author-tex: |
  \author{
    Sebastian Nagl\textsuperscript{1}\thanks{\,Core Contributor, corresponding author. Correspondence to \texttt{sebastian.nagl@tum.de}.} \quad
    Ann-Kristin Mayrhofer\textsuperscript{2}\thanks{\,Dataset contributor.} \quad
    Martin Heidebach\textsuperscript{2\textdagger} \\
    \bfseries Aleyna Koçak\textsuperscript{3\textdagger} \quad
    Anne Zettelmeier\textsuperscript{4\textdagger} \quad
    Elly Breu\textsuperscript{1\textdagger} \\
    \bfseries Angelina Greiner\textsuperscript{1\textdagger} \quad
    Sofija Milijas\textsuperscript{1\textdagger} \quad
    Matthias Grabmair\textsuperscript{1}\\[6pt]
    \mdseries \textsuperscript{1}Technical University of Munich (TUM) \quad
    \textsuperscript{2}Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich (LMU) \\
    \textsuperscript{3}University of Konstanz \quad
    \textsuperscript{4}University of Saarbrücken 
  }
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: false

import json
from pathlib import Path
from collections import Counter

def load_json(path):
    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        return json.load(f)

def load_optional(path):
    try:
        return load_json(path)
    except FileNotFoundError:
        return None

interim   = Path("data/interim")
raw       = Path("data/raw")
processed = Path("data/processed")

zjs = load_json(interim / "zjs.json")
grundprinzipien = load_json(interim / "grundprinzipien.json")
benchathon = load_json(interim / "benchathon.json")
variants = load_json(processed / "benchathon_instruction_variants.json")
benchathon_tasks = load_json(raw / "benchathon" / "Benchathon_export.json")["tasks"]

# Derived exports - produced by scripts/derive_paper_exports.py from the real
# Benchathon export + scripts/derive_inter_judge_agreement.py.
# Loaded if present so the manuscript still renders before derive has run.
systems_meta        = load_optional(processed / "systems.json")
model_evals         = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_model_evaluations.json")
human_grades        = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_human_grades.json")
automatic_metrics   = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_automatic_metrics.json")
human_judge_repeats = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_human_judge_repeats.json")
zjs_summary         = load_optional(processed / "zjs_model_summary.json")

n_zjs = len(zjs)
n_qa = len(grundprinzipien)
n_benchathon = len(benchathon)

_graded_ann_ids = {
    ev["annotation_id"]
    for t in benchathon_tasks
    for ev in t.get("evaluations", [])
    if "human:loesung" in (ev.get("field_name") or "")
    and ev.get("annotation_id") is not None
}
variant_counts = Counter(variants.get(aid) for aid in _graded_ann_ids)
n_human_only = variant_counts["no_ai"]
n_co_creation = variant_counts["ai"]
n_human_total = n_human_only + n_co_creation

# ---- Inline-expression bindings for prose: ----
agreement_stats_setup = load_optional(processed / "agreement_stats.json") or {}
r3 = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq3_metric_correlation", {})
r3_humans = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq3_metric_correlation_humans") or {}
CANONICAL_METRIC_ORDER = (
    "bleu", "rouge", "meteor", "chrf",
    "bertscore", "moverscore", "semantic_similarity",
)
METRIC_LABELS = {
    "bleu": "BLEU", "rouge": "ROUGE", "meteor": "METEOR", "chrf": "chrF",
    "bertscore": "BERTScore", "moverscore": "MoverScore",
    "semantic_similarity": "Semantic sim.",
}
r4 = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq4_cocreation", {})
r4_bereich = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq4_cocreation_by_bereich", {}) or {}
r4_tost = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq4_cocreation_vs_flagship_tost") or {}
r5_jh = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_judge_vs_human", {})
r5_irr = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_human_irr", {})
r5_irr_bal_raw = (r5_irr.get("balanced_subset") or {}).get("raw") or {}
r5_cal = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_calderon", {})
r5_dim = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_dim", {})
r5_jr = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_judge_repeats", {}) or {}
r4h = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq4_cocreation_blind_human") or {}
r5_creator = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_creator_vs_blind") or {}
r5_llm = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_judge_on_llm_solutions") or {}
r5_cal_llm = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_calderon_on_llm_solutions") or {}
r5_dim_llm = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_dim_on_llm_solutions") or {}
r5_irr_type = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_human_irr_by_solution_type") or {}
inter_judge = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_inter_judge_agreement.json") or {}
tier_agg = agreement_stats_setup.get("tier_aggregates") or {}
rank_corr = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq3_rank_correlation") or {}
zjs_metric_corr = load_optional(processed / "zjs_metric_correlation.json") or {}
grundprinzipien_summary  = load_optional(processed / "grundprinzipien_model_summary.json") or []
grundprinzipien_metric_corr = load_optional(processed / "grundprinzipien_metric_correlation.json") or {}
grundprinzipien_tier_agg = load_optional(processed / "grundprinzipien_tier_aggregates.json") or {}
error_analysis = load_optional(processed / "error_analysis.json") or {}
judge_swap = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_judge_swap.json") or {}
reasoning_core = load_optional(processed / "reasoning_core_leaderboard.json") or {}
metric_vs_human = load_optional(processed / "metric_vs_human.json") or {}
operational_meta = load_optional(processed / "operational_metadata.json") or {}
reasoning_ablation = load_optional(processed / "reasoning_ablation.json") or {}

def _ra_v(corpus, variant, field):
    """Safe deep-get for the reasoning-mode ablation stats."""
    return (((reasoning_ablation.get("corpora") or {}).get(corpus) or {})
            .get(variant) or {}).get(field)

def _mvh(metric, split="pooled", field="pearson_r", block="metric_vs_human"):
    """Safe deep-get for the metric-vs-human-grade analysis."""
    return (((metric_vs_human.get(block) or {}).get(metric) or {})
            .get(split) or {}).get(field)

def _jswap_rank(field="spearman_rho"):
    """Cross-judge system-level rank agreement values (excl. the primary's
    trivial self-comparison)."""
    ra = judge_swap.get("system_rank_agreement") or {}
    primary = (judge_swap.get("_meta") or {}).get("primary_judge")
    return [v.get(field) for k, v in ra.items()
            if k != primary and v.get(field) is not None]

def _rcore(corpus, field="spearman_rho"):
    """Reasoning-core vs full-rubric rank agreement per corpus."""
    return ((reasoning_core.get("rank_agreement") or {}).get(corpus) or {}).get(field)

def _op_run_dates():
    """Formatted per-corpus generation run-date ranges from logged metadata."""
    rd = operational_meta.get("run_date_ranges") or {}
    parts = []
    for key, label in (("benchathon", "Benchathon"), ("zjs", "ZJS"),
                       ("grundprinzipien", "Doctrinal Principles")):
        ranges = rd.get(key) or {}
        if ranges:
            first = min(v["first"] for v in ranges.values())
            last = max(v["last"] for v in ranges.values())
            parts.append(f"{label}: {first} to {last}.")
        else:
            parts.append(f"{label}: —.")
    return " ".join(parts)

def _op_total_retries():
    pc = operational_meta.get("per_corpus") or {}
    return sum((v.get("total_retries") or 0)
               for c in pc.values() for v in c.values())

def _zmc(metric, field="pearson_vs_judge_raw"):
    """Safe deep-get for ZJS per-generation metric correlation."""
    return (zjs_metric_corr.get(metric) or {}).get(field)

def _gmc(metric, field="pearson_vs_judge_raw"):
    """Safe deep-get for Grundprinzipien per-generation metric correlation."""
    return (grundprinzipien_metric_corr.get(metric) or {}).get(field)

def _ta(corpus, dim, key, field):
    """Safe deep-get for tier_aggregates so the prose tolerates missing keys.
    `corpus` is benchathon/zjs (sourced from agreement_stats.json) or
    grundprinzipien (sourced from the standalone tier file)."""
    if corpus == "grundprinzipien":
        return ((grundprinzipien_tier_agg.get(dim) or {}).get(key) or {}).get(field)
    return (((tier_agg.get(corpus) or {}).get(dim) or {}).get(key) or {}).get(field)

def _rc(corpus, metric, field="spearman_metric_vs_judge"):
    """Safe deep-get for rq3_rank_correlation."""
    return ((rank_corr.get(corpus) or {}).get(metric) or {}).get(field)

def fmt(x, w=2):
    return "-" if x is None else f"{x:.{w}f}"
def fmtp(x, w=2):
    return "-" if x is None else f"{x:+.{w}f}"
def fmtpct(x, w=0):
    return "-" if x is None else f"{x*100:.{w}f}%"

# Error-analysis stats (scripts/derive_error_analysis.py), per corpus
# (benchathon / zjs / grundprinzipien). None-safe so prose renders before the run.
def _eaN(corpus):
    n = (error_analysis.get(corpus) or {}).get("n")
    return "-" if n is None else f"{n:,}"
def _eac(corpus, key):
    c = (((error_analysis.get(corpus) or {}).get("modes") or {}).get(key) or {}).get("count")
    return "-" if c is None else str(c)
def _ear(corpus, key, w=1):
    c = (((error_analysis.get(corpus) or {}).get("modes") or {}).get(key) or {}).get("count")
    n = (error_analysis.get(corpus) or {}).get("n")
    return "-" if (c is None or not n) else f"{100*c/n:.{w}f}%"
def _eacoup(corpus, field):
    return fmt((error_analysis.get(corpus) or {}).get(field), 2)
def _eaAcc(corpus):
    return fmtpct((error_analysis.get(corpus) or {}).get("accuracy"), 1)
def _eart(corpus, key, tier, w=1):
    """Per-tier rate of a mode (tier in flagship/efficiency/open_reference)."""
    bt = (((error_analysis.get(corpus) or {}).get("modes") or {}).get(key) or {}).get("by_tier") or {}
    r = (bt.get(tier) or {}).get("rate")
    return "-" if r is None else f"{100*r:.{w}f}%"
def _eaAccT(corpus, tier):
    a = ((error_analysis.get(corpus) or {}).get("accuracy_by_tier") or {}).get(tier) or {}
    return fmtpct(a.get("accuracy"), 1)
def _eaov(corpus, field):
    return (error_analysis.get(corpus) or {}).get(field)

# Convenience handles into the alt-test payload. `bp` = blind-reviewer
# pool, `se` = single-expert reference (creator un-blind grade) keyed
# by ε. None-safe so the prose still renders before the script is run.
r5_cal_bp     = (r5_cal     or {}).get("blind_pool") or {}
r5_cal_se     = ((r5_cal    or {}).get("single_expert") or {}).get("epsilon_results") or {}
r5_cal_llm_bp = (r5_cal_llm or {}).get("blind_pool") or {}
r5_cal_llm_se = ((r5_cal_llm or {}).get("single_expert") or {}).get("epsilon_results") or {}

# Reviewer-requested supplementary judge-calibration data (Shapiro-Wilk +
# paired t-test on pool-substitution differences). Per-judge / per-pass rows.
judge_calib_path = processed / "judge_calibration.json"
judge_calib = load_optional(judge_calib_path) or []
def _calib(label_match):
    """Find the first calibration row whose label contains label_match."""
    for r in judge_calib:
        if label_match in r.get("label", ""):
            return r
    return {}

# Per-judge Calderon alt-test results (rq5_calderon_per_judge.py output).
# Keys (six judges, human & LLM picks):
#   baseline_human / baseline_llm        — primary GPT-5.4-mini
#   cfgB_opus_human / cfgB_opus_llm      — Opus-4.7
#   cfgB_gemini_human / cfgB_gemini_llm  — Gemini-3.1-Pro
#   deepseek_human / deepseek_llm        — DeepSeek-V4-Pro
#   qwen_human / qwen_llm                — Qwen3.5-397B-A17B
#   sonnet_human / sonnet_llm            — Sonnet-4.6
calderon_per_judge = load_optional(processed / "calderon_per_judge.json") or {}
def _cpj(key):
    return (calderon_per_judge.get(key) or {}).get("payload") or {}

def _pass(d):
    if not d:
        return "-"
    return "passes" if d.get("passes_alt_test") else "does not pass"


# Pre-extracted dimension values (the inline-expression form
# `r5_dim.get('x', {}).get('y')` has historically tripped Quarto's
# markdown lexer because of the empty-dict literal `{}`).
def _dim(dim_name, key):
    return ((r5_dim or {}).get(dim_name) or {}).get(key)

dim_pearson_ergebnis      = _dim("ergebnisrichtigkeit", "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_vollstaendig  = _dim("vollstaendigkeit",    "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_rechtsgr      = _dim("rechtsgrundlagen",    "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_rechtsk       = _dim("rechtskenntnis",      "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_subsum        = _dim("subsumtion",          "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_schwerpunkt   = _dim("schwerpunktsetzung",  "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_method        = _dim("methodischer_stil",   "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_gliederung    = _dim("gliederung",          "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_sprache       = _dim("sprache",             "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_formalia      = _dim("formalia",            "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_mae_ergebnis          = _dim("ergebnisrichtigkeit", "mae_dim_points")
dim_mae_formalia          = _dim("formalia",            "mae_dim_points")

# Friendly labels for the rubric dimensions, in the same casing as the prose.
_dim_label = {
    "ergebnisrichtigkeit": "Result correctness",
    "vollstaendigkeit":    "Issue identification",
    "rechtsgrundlagen":    "Legal grounding",
    "rechtskenntnis":      "Doctrinal knowledge",
    "subsumtion":          "Subsumption quality",
    "schwerpunktsetzung":  "Problem depth",
    "methodischer_stil":   "Methodological structure",
    "gliederung":          "Organization",
    "sprache":             "Terminology",
    "formalia":            "Formal correctness",
}

# Sorted dim Pearson list - drives the "highest on / lowest on" prose in RQ5.
_dim_pearson_sorted = sorted(
    (
        (label, _dim(dim, "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human"))
        for dim, label in _dim_label.items()
    ),
    key=lambda kv: (kv[1] if kv[1] is not None else float("-inf")),
    reverse=True,
)
def _dim_prose_top(n):
    items = [(lbl, v) for lbl, v in _dim_pearson_sorted[:n] if v is not None]
    return ", ".join(f"{lbl} (r={fmt(v)})" for lbl, v in items)
def _dim_prose_bottom(n):
    items = [(lbl, v) for lbl, v in _dim_pearson_sorted[-n:] if v is not None]
    return ", ".join(f"{lbl} (r={fmt(v)})" for lbl, v in items)

# Short alias used throughout the paper for each model id; full ids stay
# in `Table~\ref{tbl-system-overview}`{=latex} (Appendix B) so the body prose can reference
# systems compactly. Keep this dict in sync with the systems_meta names.
MODEL_ALIAS = {
    "claude-sonnet-4-6":                              "Sonnet-4.6",
    "claude-opus-4-7":                                "Opus-4.7",
    "gpt-5.4":                                        "GPT-5.4",
    "gpt-5.4-mini":                                   "GPT-5.4-mini",
    "gpt-5-mini":                                     "GPT-5-mini",
    "gemini-3.1-pro-preview":                         "Gemini-3.1-Pro",
    "gemini-3-flash-preview":                         "Gemini-3-Flash",
    "gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview":                  "Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite",
    "deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Pro":                    "DeepSeek-V4-Pro",
    "deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Flash":                  "DeepSeek-V4-Flash",
    "Qwen/Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507":             "Qwen3-235B",
    "Qwen/Qwen3.5-122B-A10B":                         "Qwen3.5-122B",
    "Qwen/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B":                           "Qwen3.6-35B",
    "meta-llama/Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8": "Llama-4",
    # systems.json strips provider prefixes from open-weight names; alias both forms.
    "Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507":                  "Qwen3-235B",
    "Qwen3.5-122B-A10B":                              "Qwen3.5-122B",
    "Qwen3.6-35B-A3B":                                "Qwen3.6-35B",
    "Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8":         "Llama-4",
}

def alias_for(model_id):
    """Return the short alias for a model id, falling back to the id itself."""
    return MODEL_ALIAS.get(model_id, model_id)

# Per-system mean judge raw on Benchathon (for RQ1 prose).
from statistics import mean as _mean
bench_by_sys = {}
if model_evals:
    _acc = {}
    for r in model_evals:
        if r.get("raw_score") is not None:
            _acc.setdefault(r["system"], []).append(r["raw_score"])
    bench_by_sys = {k: _mean(v) for k, v in _acc.items()}

zjs_by_sys = {}
if zjs_summary:
    for r in zjs_summary:
        zjs_by_sys[r["system"]] = r["metrics"]

grundprinzipien_by_sys = {}
for r in grundprinzipien_summary:
    grundprinzipien_by_sys[r["system"]] = r["metrics"]

def b(sys_id):
    """Mean Benchathon judge raw for a system id, or None."""
    return bench_by_sys.get(sys_id)
def z(sys_id, key="llm_judge_falloesung_raw_mean"):
    return (zjs_by_sys.get(sys_id) or {}).get(key)
def g(sys_id, key="judge_raw_mean"):
    """Per-system Grundprinzipien metric (judge_raw_mean / judge_pass_rate /
    accuracy / <metric>_mean), 0-100 for judge raw, 0-1 for accuracy."""
    return (grundprinzipien_by_sys.get(sys_id) or {}).get(key)

def placeholder_panel(figsize, title, missing_files, hint):
    import matplotlib.pyplot as _plt
    fig, ax = _plt.subplots(figsize=figsize)
    ax.axis("off")
    body = f"{title}\n\nPending export(s):\n  • " + "\n  • ".join(missing_files) + f"\n\n{hint}"
    ax.text(0.02, 0.98, body, ha="left", va="top", fontsize=7,
            family="monospace", color="#444",
            bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.5", fc="#fff8dc", ec="#d4a017"))
    _plt.tight_layout(pad=0.3)
    return fig, ax

# Per-condition human-judge scores on Benchathon (raws, passed). Used by the
# RQ4 fig-human-vs-ai chunk (body) and the main leaderboard chunk; hoisted
# into setup so the figure can run before the leaderboard chunk in source
# order.
_human_judge_by_variant = {"no_ai": ([], []), "ai": ([], [])}
# Only include scores from the primary GPT-5.4-mini single-pass run
# (BASELINE_JUDGE_FIELD_PREFIX = llm_judge_falloesung-mptmfvee). Other
# llm_judge_falloesung-* prefixes correspond to cross-validation judges
# (Opus, Gemini, DeepSeek, Qwen, Sonnet) or Config-A intra-judge passes;
# mixing those into the human-baseline rows would average across judges.
_PRIMARY_JUDGE_PREFIX = "llm_judge_falloesung-mptmfvee"
for _t in benchathon_tasks:
    for _ev in _t.get("evaluations") or []:
        _fn = _ev.get("field_name") or ""
        if not _fn.startswith(_PRIMARY_JUDGE_PREFIX) or "human:loesung" not in _fn:
            continue
        _m = _ev.get("metrics") or {}
        # GPT-5.4-mini run uses Shape A (nested under `llm_judge_falloesung`);
        # the legacy single-pass GPT-5-mini run used Shape B (flat keys).
        _judge = _m.get("llm_judge_falloesung")
        _details = _judge.get("details") if isinstance(_judge, dict) else None
        _rs = (_details or {}).get("raw_score") if _details else _m.get("llm_judge_falloesung_raw")
        _ps = (_details or {}).get("passed") if _details else _m.get("llm_judge_falloesung_passed")
        if _rs is None:
            continue
        _v = variants.get(_ev.get("annotation_id"))
        if _v not in _human_judge_by_variant:
            continue
        _human_judge_by_variant[_v][0].append(float(_rs))
        _human_judge_by_variant[_v][1].append(bool(_ps))

# Count-consistency guard: the Dataset-section counts, the leaderboard human
# rows, and the RQ4 per-arm n must all agree on 220 = 65 + 155. The ARR
# submission shipped with three divergent count families (220/65/155 vs
# 226/66/160 vs 72/156) because these were derived independently; fail the
# render loudly if they ever drift again.
_lb_trad = len(_human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][0])
_lb_ai = len(_human_judge_by_variant["ai"][0])
assert n_human_total == n_human_only + n_co_creation, (
    f"dataset-section counts inconsistent: {n_human_total} != "
    f"{n_human_only} + {n_co_creation}")
assert (_lb_trad, _lb_ai) == (n_human_only, n_co_creation), (
    f"leaderboard human rows ({_lb_trad}/{_lb_ai}) disagree with "
    f"dataset-section counts ({n_human_only}/{n_co_creation})")
assert (r4.get("n_traditional"), r4.get("n_co_creation")) == (n_human_only, n_co_creation), (
    f"RQ4 per-arm n ({r4.get('n_traditional')}/{r4.get('n_co_creation')}) disagrees "
    f"with dataset-section counts ({n_human_only}/{n_co_creation})")

# Static LaTeX floats written to assets/ and \input at their anchors —
# same routing-around-Quarto pattern as tbl_main_leaderboard (a raw
# ```{=latex} table block with a \caption gets mangled by Quarto's
# crossref pass; \input{} one-liners are inert).
from pathlib import Path as _APath
_APath("assets").mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

_ = _APath("assets/tbl_rubric_mapping.tex").write_text(r"""\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\footnotesize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}
\begin{tabular}{@{}p{0.58\columnwidth}p{0.32\columnwidth}@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Dimension (weight / 100)} & \textbf{Scaffold step graded} \\
\midrule
Result correctness -- \emph{Ergebnisrichtigkeit} (20) & \emph{Ergebnis} \\
Doctrinal knowledge -- \emph{Rechtskenntnis} (15) & \emph{Definition} \\
Subsumption quality -- \emph{Subsumtion} (15) & \emph{Subsumtion} \\
Issue identification -- \emph{Vollständigkeit} (10) & \emph{Obersatz} (issue spotting) \\
Legal grounding -- \emph{Rechtsgrundlagen} (10) & \emph{Obersatz} (norm citation) \\
Problem depth -- \emph{Schwerpunktsetzung} (10) & \emph{Subsumtion} (focal-issue depth) \\
Methodological structure -- \emph{Methodischer Stil} (10) & whole-scaffold execution \\
Organization -- \emph{Gliederung} (5) & form \\
Terminology -- \emph{Sprache} (3) & form \\
Formal correctness -- \emph{Formalia} (2) & form \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Rubric dimensions mapped to the \emph{Gutachtenstil} scaffold step each grades, with /100 weights from the judge prompt (Appendix~\ref{evaluation-prompts-for-the-llm-judge}). The judge's global principle additionally gates all credit on the method: ``Keine Punkte für bloße Schlagworte ohne Definition/Subsumtion'' (no points for mere buzzwords without definition/subsumption).}
\label{tbl-rubric-mapping}
\end{table}
""", encoding="utf-8")

_GLOSSARY_ROWS = [
    (r"\emph{Falllösung}", "Written case solution -- the end-to-end work product that is graded."),
    (r"\emph{Gutachtenstil}", "Appraisal style: the conclusion follows the reasoning; the register graded here."),
    (r"\emph{Urteilsstil}", "Judgment style: result first, reasoning after; used in judicial decisions."),
    (r"\emph{Obersatz}", "Step 1 of the scaffold: the norm hypothesis to be examined."),
    (r"\emph{Definition}", "Step 2: interpretation of the norm's elements."),
    (r"\emph{Subsumtion}", "Step 3: anchoring the concrete facts under the norm's elements; broadly, the entire norm-application method."),
    (r"\emph{Ergebnis}", "Step 4: the resulting legal consequence."),
    (r"\emph{Musterlösung}", "Expert reference solution against which submissions are graded."),
    (r"\emph{Staatsexamen}", "State law examination; gate to all regulated legal professions in Germany."),
    (r"\emph{ausreichend}", "``Sufficient'' -- lowest passing grade (4--6 of 18 points)."),
    (r"\emph{vollbefriedigend}", "``Fully satisfactory'' (10--12 points); customary distinction tier (\\emph{Prädikat})."),
    (r"\emph{Referendar}", "Legal trainee in the two-year practical stage between the state examinations."),
    (r"\emph{Volljurist}", "Fully qualified jurist (passed both state examinations)."),
    (r"\emph{Grundprinzipien}", "Core doctrinal principles; German name of the Doctrinal Principles corpus."),
    (r"\emph{Bereich(e)}", "Legal area(s): civil, public, criminal law."),
    ("Ja/Nein", "Yes/no decision label on Doctrinal Principles items."),
    (r"\emph{Ergebnisrichtigkeit}", "Rubric dimension: result correctness."),
    (r"\emph{Vollständigkeit}", "Rubric dimension: issue identification / completeness."),
    (r"\emph{Rechtsgrundlagen}", "Rubric dimension: legal grounding (norm citation)."),
    (r"\emph{Rechtskenntnis}", "Rubric dimension: doctrinal knowledge."),
    (r"\emph{Schwerpunktsetzung}", "Rubric dimension: problem depth at the case's focal issues."),
    (r"\emph{Methodischer Stil}", "Rubric dimension: methodological structure (scaffold execution)."),
    (r"\emph{Gliederung}", "Rubric dimension: organization."),
    (r"\emph{Sprache}", "Rubric dimension: terminology and language."),
    (r"\emph{Formalia}", "Rubric dimension: formal correctness."),
]
_gloss_body = "\n".join(f"{t} & {g} \\\\" for t, g in _GLOSSARY_ROWS)
_ = _APath("assets/tbl_glossary.tex").write_text(
    "\\begin{table*}[t]\n\\centering\n\\footnotesize\n"
    "\\setlength{\\tabcolsep}{5pt}\n"
    "\\begin{tabular}{@{}p{0.2\\textwidth}p{0.75\\textwidth}@{}}\n"
    "\\toprule\n\\textbf{Term} & \\textbf{Gloss} \\\\\n\\midrule\n"
    + _gloss_body +
    "\n\\bottomrule\n\\end{tabular}\n"
    "\\caption{German terms used in the paper, with English glosses.}\n"
    "\\label{tbl-glossary}\n\\end{table*}\n",
    encoding="utf-8")
```

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: only the opening changes for blind mode.
Replace "We introduce BenGER (Benchmark for German Law), a benchmark and
dataset for subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law." with the
de-identified form "We introduce a benchmark and dataset for subsumption-
based legal reasoning in German law." - keep the `\begin{abstract}` /
`\end{abstract}` LaTeX wrappers in both modes; the ACL formatter requires
this environment, not a markdown heading. -->
```{=latex}
\begin{abstract}
```

We introduce BenGER (Benchmark for German Law), a benchmark and dataset for subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law. It combines `{python} f"{n_zjs + n_benchathon}"` exam-style free-text case tasks across several levels of legal education with `{python} f"{n_qa}"` short doctrinal reasoning tasks. A controlled subset adds timed human solutions, written both unaided and in human-AI co-creation. We evaluate `{python} f"{len(systems_meta) if systems_meta else 12}"` contemporary LLM systems: closed flagship, efficiency-oriented, and open-weight. A rubric-aligned LLM-as-a-Judge scores them. We cross-validate it against a multi-rater human layer (three blind reviews per solution) and across six LLM judges. Closed-flagship systems lead across all three corpora. Human-AI co-creation measurably improves on unaided human work. The judge tracks human grading at Pearson r=`{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('pearson_raw'),2)` and Cohen's κ=`{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('passfail_cohen_kappa'),2)`. Rankings are stable across judges. Two judges from independent providers clear the Calderon single-reviewer replacement bar on human-authored solutions.
```{=latex}
\end{abstract}
```


```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

# Emit the fig-overview float here, very early in the body, so that LaTeX's
# \afterpage hook fires after page 1 ships out -- placing the figure at the
# top of page 2. The matplotlib PDF itself is generated by the chunk further
# down (after the Introduction); chunk execution order is source order, but
# the PDF file is on disk before LaTeX runs either way. Keep this chunk in
# sync with the figure builder below.
#
# Because the float is emitted as raw LaTeX (not a Quarto-native figure chunk),
# Quarto's `@fig-overview` crossref does NOT resolve to it. Body prose
# references this figure as `Figure~\ref{fig-overview}`{=latex} -- every other
# figure in the paper uses Quarto-native `@fig-...` crossrefs.
print(
    r"\afterpage{%" "\n"
    r"\begin{figure*}[t!]" "\n"
    r"\centering" "\n"
    r"\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{assets/fig-overview.png}" "\n"
    r"\caption{Overview of the benchmark and evaluation pipeline. "
    r"Three German-law corpora -- the published ZJS exam corpus, the short "
    r"Doctrinal Principles question set, and the controlled Benchathon "
    r"validation subset with human solutions in two working conditions -- "
    r"are answered by a panel of LLM systems and scored by a rubric-aligned "
    r"LLM judge. The Benchathon subset is additionally graded by a blind "
    r"human reviewer pool (with one author-informed creator review per "
    r"solution); the resulting human distribution is used to validate the "
    r"LLM judge.}" "\n"
    r"\label{fig-overview}" "\n"
    r"\end{figure*}%" "\n"
    r"}" "\n"
)
```

# Introduction

Evaluating legal reasoning is hard. Even human experts grade the same answer very differently. Hufeld [@hufeld2024] shows that identical answers in German law exams receive markedly different scores depending on the grader. Doctrinal analysis is interpretive, so this is no surprise. But it is a problem for benchmarking large language models (LLMs). If human evaluation is itself noisy, what does it mean to benchmark legal reasoning?

Legal NLP benchmarks have evolved fast. They moved from classification [@chalkidis2022lexglue; @guha2023legalbench] to reasoning [@fei2024lawbench; @fan2026lexam; @jia2025readyjuristone; @shi2026plawbench] and retrieval-augmented evaluation [@pipitone2024legalbenchrag; @butler2026legalragbench]. But most still emphasise short-form QA, common-law jurisdictions, or stable ground-truth labels. Open-ended subsumption-based German legal reasoning under noisy expert judgement remains under-served.

We address these gaps with a benchmark centered on subsumption-based legal reasoning, the core reasoning paradigm in German law (@sec-legal-methodology) and, more broadly, in many civil-law jurisdictions worldwide.

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: replace this paragraph with the de-identified form:
"We present a benchmark^[Full code and dataset will be released upon paper
acceptance.] for evaluating LLMs on subsumption-based legal reasoning in
German law. It has three components. Together they cover
`{python} f"{n_zjs + n_benchathon}"` exam-style free-text case tasks across
different stages of legal education and `{python} f"{n_qa}"` short doctrinal
reasoning tasks. It also adds human baselines, both traditional legal work
and human-AI co-creation, on a controlled Benchathon validation subset.
`Figure~\ref{fig-overview}`{=latex} summarises the three components and the
generation-plus-evaluation pipeline."
(Drop the "BenGER" name, drop the GitHub URL, drop the platform
self-citation sentence.) -->
We present the BenGER (Benchmark for German Law) dataset^[Full code and dataset are available at <https://github.com/SebastianNagl/benger-platform>.] for evaluating LLMs on subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law. It has three components. Together they cover `{python} f"{n_zjs + n_benchathon}"` exam-style free-text case tasks across different stages of legal education and `{python} f"{n_qa}"` short doctrinal reasoning tasks. It also adds human baselines, both traditional legal work and human-AI co-creation, on a controlled Benchathon validation subset. `Figure~\ref{fig-overview}`{=latex} summarises the three components and the generation-plus-evaluation pipeline. Generation, annotation, and evaluation ran end-to-end on the BenGER platform [@nagl2026benger], the open-source web application we built earlier for benchmarking legal-domain LLM systems.

How do we evaluate free-text reasoning when the human judgement is itself noisy? We adopt a hybrid framework. An LLM-as-a-Judge, aligned with German grading rubrics, does the scoring. We cross-validate it against three blind human reviews per solution, plus an author-informed creator review as a reference signal. We treat evaluation as a distribution over plausible expert assessments, not a single deterministic label.

We make three contributions. (i) A benchmark for subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law. (ii) An empirical evaluation of `{python} f"{len(systems_meta) if systems_meta else 12}"` contemporary LLM systems – closed flagship, efficiency-oriented, and open-weight – alongside human and co-creative baselines. (iii) An evaluation methodology that accounts for the inherent variability of legal grading.

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: drop "of the full BenGER dataset" so the sentence
reads "We organise the empirical analysis around five questions." -->
We organise the empirical analysis of the full BenGER dataset around five questions. **RQ1 (Performance):** how well do contemporary LLM systems solve open-ended subsumption-based German legal reasoning tasks, and how do they compare to human baselines? **RQ2 (System-tier effects):** how does performance vary across system tiers and open-/closed-weight access modes? **RQ3 (Evaluation validity):** how well do generic automatic metrics correlate with rubric-based legal evaluation? **RQ4 (Human-AI co-creation):** does AI-assisted drafting improve human performance, and how does it compare to standalone LLM systems? **RQ5 (Grading reliability):** how closely do LLM-judge evaluations align with the empirical distribution of human grades, and are their deviations comparable to inter-rater variability?

# Subsumption-based legal reasoning {#sec-legal-methodology}

Subsumption is the core reasoning paradigm of German civil-law doctrine [@larenz1995methodenlehre; @moellers2023methodenlehre]. Common-law reasoning proceeds through analogy to precedent. German analysis works differently. It derives outcomes from codified statutory norms through a fixed four-step scaffold: *Obersatz* (the norm hypothesis to be examined), *Definition* (interpretation of the norm's elements), *Subsumtion* (anchoring the concrete facts under those elements)^[*Subsumtion* carries a broad and a narrow sense. Broadly, it names the entire norm-application method – the sense in the paper title. Narrowly, it names the single fact-anchoring step of the scaffold – the sense used for the scaffold step and the rubric dimension of the same name.], and *Ergebnis* (the resulting legal consequence). The scaffold is the German articulation of the legal syllogism that underlies statutory reasoning across civil-law jurisdictions; what is Germany-specific is its rubric-graded written form, not the reasoning operation itself. This is the *Gutachtenstil*, the opinion style in which the conclusion follows the reasoning. It differs from the result-first *Urteilsstil* used in judicial decisions. German legal education, state examinations, and entry-level practice are all trained and assessed in this style. Grading targets the written *Falllösung* (case solution) end-to-end, not the final answer alone. The rubric rewards how each norm element is anchored in the specific facts.

This makes subsumption a structurally demanding generation task. A system must identify the governing norm, articulate its elements, anchor every element in the facts, and conclude. Surface fluency can mask a missing *Subsumtion* step. But the same structure makes the task unusually amenable to rubric-based evaluation. Each step in the scaffold maps to an identifiable part of the answer, not to a global quality impression. English-language legal NLP benchmarks built on classification, multiple-choice, or precedent-style reasoning [@chalkidis2022lexglue; @guha2023legalbench] do not exercise this scaffold. German terms are glossed at first use throughout; a consolidated glossary is in Appendix `\ref{glossary-of-german-terms}`{=latex}.

# Related work

Legal NLP benchmarking has moved from classification to reasoning. Early baselines were LexGLUE [@chalkidis2022lexglue] (seven English legal datasets) and LegalBench [@guha2023legalbench] (162 mostly short-form US-federal-law tasks). Recent work targets jurisdiction-specific reasoning: LawBench [@fei2024lawbench] and LexEval [@li2024lexeval] for Chinese civil law, KBL [@kim2024kbl] for Korean bar-exam questions, LAR-ECHR [@chlapanis2024larechr] for European Court of Human Rights argument chains, KOBLEX [@lee2025koblex] for provision-grounded multi-hop reasoning in Korean law, GreekBarBench [@chlapanis2025greekbarbench] for free-text Greek bar exams, NitiBench [@akarajaradwong2025nitibench] for Thai financial and tax law, PLawBench [@shi2026plawbench] for rubric-graded Chinese legal practice across 13 practical scenarios, and LaborBench [@hariri2025laborbench] for US state labor-law statutory simplification. A complementary line targets retrieval-augmented evaluation, LegalBench-RAG [@pipitone2024legalbenchrag] and Legal RAG Bench [@butler2026legalragbench], and discrepancy-based auditing of LLM legal reasoning, CLAUSE [@choudhury2026clause]. The closest precedent is LEXam [@fan2026lexam]: 7,537 questions across 340 law exams in English and German, with an ensemble LLM-as-a-Judge validated against human experts. But its rubric is not the *Obersatz-Definition-Subsumtion-Ergebnis* scaffold central to German *Falllösung* grading (@sec-legal-methodology). And it does not couple its free-text items to the dimension-level grading criteria used in German law school and state-exam evaluation.

Free-text legal evaluation increasingly relies on LLM-as-a-Judge approaches. These raise concerns over bias, calibration, and alignment with expert judgement. German legal education shows how variable human grading already is: when Hufeld [@hufeld2024] had 23 graders score 15 beginner-level law exams, the average range between the lowest and highest grade per answer was 6.47 points on the 0-18 scale, and only 42% of grades fell within ±1 point of the answer-specific mean. Legal grading is therefore better treated as a distribution over plausible expert judgements than as a single ground truth, which motivates hybrid methods that combine human expertise with calibrated LLM-based scoring.

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: replace "Our prior BenGER platform [@nagl2026benger]
provides an end-to-end framework for German legal case analysis and
subsumption-based reasoning workflows, on which the present dataset was
built." with the non-self-attributing form "The BenGER platform
[@nagl2026benger] provides an end-to-end framework for German legal case
analysis and subsumption-based reasoning workflows.", and replace "The
BenGER dataset addresses this gap." with "Our benchmark addresses this
gap.". -->
Resources for German legal NLP remain limited. GerLayQA [@buttner2024gerlayqa] provides ~21,000 lay questions paired with lawyer answers, but focuses on QA rather than subsumption. GerLeRB [@weber2025gerlerb] targets legislative retrieval. Our prior BenGER platform [@nagl2026benger] provides an end-to-end framework for German legal case analysis and subsumption-based reasoning workflows, on which the present dataset was built. German reasoning grounded in doctrinal subsumption under statutory law remains under-served, especially for open-ended, structured analysis under noisy human judgement. The BenGER dataset addresses this gap. It combines a `{python} f"{n_zjs + n_benchathon}"`-task exam-style corpus and `{python} f"{n_qa}"` short doctrinal reasoning items with a controlled Benchathon validation subset, a German doctrinal rubric, and a multi-rater cross-validation protocol.

# Dataset

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: "The BenGER dataset draws on three components" →
"Our benchmark draws on three corpora". -->
The BenGER dataset draws on three components. They cover the main forms of legal reasoning in German legal education. Two are exam-style case corpora: a fact pattern is given, and a structured *Falllösung* is expected. The third is a set of short doctrinal reasoning items, probing core principles without the full case-analysis scaffolding.

Only the first component builds on pre-existing material. The ZJS corpus contributes `{python} n_zjs` publicly available exam-style cases from the *Zeitschrift für das Juristische Studium* (Journal for the Study of Law)^[<https://www.zjs-online.com/>]. They cover civil, criminal, and public law. They span early undergraduate exercises through first and selected second state-examination materials. Each is paired with an expert-written reference solution. The cases and reference solutions pre-exist this work as journal publications; our contribution is the benchmark artifact: selection, machine-readable task-reference pairs, per-item IP clearance (§Ethical considerations). The Benchathon corpus adds `{python} n_benchathon` newly collected exam-style tasks at an intermediate difficulty level, more demanding than introductory exercises but less complex than full first-state-exam problems. The tasks and their reference solutions were authored for this benchmark by the domain experts on the author team (the task authors of Appendix `\ref{full-benchathon-human-evaluation-assignment-procedure}`{=latex}). It comprises `{python} n_human_total` human solutions in total (`{python} n_human_only` traditional, `{python} n_co_creation` human-AI co-creation; about `{python} f"{round(n_human_total / n_benchathon)}"` per task).^[The corpus is named after the in-person Benchathon event at which participants produced these solutions and the validation human grading. Participants were predominantly law students at various stages of legal training, plus a smaller group of *Referendare* (post-state-exam legal trainees), recent graduates, and a small layperson cohort; full composition in Appendix `\ref{benchathon-participant-composition}`{=latex}.] The Doctrinal Principles corpus (*Grundprinzipien*) contributes `{python} n_qa` short doctrinal reasoning items – question-answer pairs with explanatory reasoning – on core principles, with recent case law where appropriate. The items were newly written for this benchmark by a subset of the authors with the relevant domain expertise and, like Benchathon, were unpublished prior to this work. The three play distinct roles. ZJS supports at-scale automated evaluation. Benchathon supports the human baseline and judge-validation layer. Doctrinal Principles probes the same reasoning at shorter form.

The benchmark is for evaluation only. We do not define a train/test split. The objective is comparative evaluation of deployed LLM systems and human baselines. Pretraining-contamination considerations across the three corpora are discussed in §Limitations.

## Benchathon task design and human solution collection

Benchathon participants solved tasks in a controlled annotation environment. Each task was randomly assigned to one of two conditions. In the *traditional* condition, participants could use statutory texts, legal databases, literature, and internet research. In the *co-creation* condition, they could additionally use any LLM(s) of their choosing – commercial or open-weight, in any combination – through their own accounts. We did not log which tool a participant used. The co-creation arm therefore measures an upper bound on what human-LLM teams currently achieve with off-the-shelf assistance, not a controlled comparison against any specific system. Only the final written solution is evaluated, mirroring legal examination practice. Each task had a two-hour time limit. Participant expertise was measured objectively (reported legal grades and qualifications) and subjectively (self-assessed competence on a Likert scale).

# Experimental setup

## Generation

We evaluate `{python} f"{len(systems_meta) if systems_meta else 12}"` contemporary LLM systems: 6 closed-weight through provider APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) and 6 open-weight through a managed inference provider (DeepInfra). Per-system providers, snapshots, observed temperatures, token-budget settings, truncation counts, and the short aliases used in the body are in `Table~\ref{tbl-system-overview}`{=latex} (`Appendix~\ref{evaluated-systems-catalogue}`{=latex}). Each task received at least one generation per system. Flagship and large-context systems received additional repetitions where capacity allowed. All systems generated the Benchathon subset. On ZJS, all `{python} f"{len(zjs_summary) if zjs_summary else 12}"` systems covered up to `{python} f"{max((r['n_tasks'] for r in zjs_summary), default=n_zjs)}"` published exams.^[Individual questions were occasionally flagged as prohibited content by the provider's safety filter, leaving a small handful of per-system coverage gaps below the corpus total.]

All systems received the same German-language system and instruction prompts (Appendix `\ref{system-prompts-for-generation}`{=latex} + `\ref{instruction-prompts-for-generation}`{=latex}). Each returned a single JSON object containing the full case solution (*Falllösung*). The instruction prompt mirrors the *Gutachtenstil* scaffold (@sec-legal-methodology). We used provider-recommended temperatures and token budgets (temperature 0.0-1.0, max output 8,000-16,000 tokens). Generations that were malformed or shorter than 200 output tokens after a bounded retry budget were excluded. Truncation in kept generations falls mostly on the smaller open-weight systems, with a few on Gemini-3.1-Pro (`Table~\ref{tbl-system-overview}`{=latex}).

## Black-box system evaluation
We evaluate LLM systems through provider APIs in a black-box setting. Our results therefore measure end-to-end system behaviour, not isolated base-model capability. We do not enable retrieval or tool-use endpoints. Components we cannot disable operate by default – notably content filters and, on some products, internal routing between sub-models. This reflects realistic deployment conditions. But it limits causal claims about underlying architectures; for open weights we test the reasoning mechanism directly with a same-base-model Thinking-vs.-Instruct toggle (Appendix `\ref{reasoning-mode-ablation}`{=latex}).

## Automatic metrics

Per generation we compute BLEU [@papineni2002bleu], ROUGE [@lin2004rouge], METEOR [@banerjee2005meteor], chrF [@popovic2015chrf], BERTScore [@zhang2020bertscore], MoverScore [@zhao2019moverscore], and a Sentence-BERT cosine similarity [@reimers2019sentencebert] against the expert reference solution. Per-system means appear in the main leaderboard tables. Per-generation values are correlated against the LLM-judge rubric score in RQ3.


## LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation
For rubric-based evaluation on ZJS and Benchathon, we use a specialised LLM-as-a-Judge prompt aligned with German legal grading practice. The judge receives the task, the reference solution, and the solution to be evaluated. It scores ten dimensions: result correctness (*Ergebnisrichtigkeit*), issue identification (*Vollständigkeit*), legal grounding (*Rechtsgrundlagen*), doctrinal knowledge (*Rechtskenntnis*), subsumption quality (*Subsumtion*), problem depth (*Schwerpunktsetzung*), methodological structure (*Methodischer Stil*), organization (*Gliederung*), terminology (*Sprache*), and formal correctness (*Formalia*). Dimension scores aggregate to a 100-point raw score, mapped to the German 0-18 grade-point scale. A generation *passes* when its grade-point score reaches *ausreichend* – ≥ 4 on the 0-18 scale, a raw score of ≥ 50/100 – following the German *Staatsexamen* (state law examination) convention.^[The ≥ 4 pass mark is statutory: the federal grading ordinance [@jurprnotskv1981] fixes *ausreichend* (4-6 of 18) as the lowest passing grade on a deliberately severe scale (≈30% fail the first state examination). The raw-to-grade conversion is non-linear – grade 4 requires ≥ 50/100 raw points (table in Appendix `\ref{evaluation-prompts-for-the-llm-judge}`{=latex}).] This is the boolean in the *Pass* columns of `Table~\ref{tbl-main-leaderboard}`{=latex} and in the pass-rate aggregates throughout. The rubric operationalises the *Gutachtenstil* scaffold (@sec-legal-methodology): each step in the *Obersatz-Definition-Subsumtion-Ergebnis* schema is reflected in a dedicated dimension (mapping and /100 weights in `Table~\ref{tbl-rubric-mapping}`{=latex}, Appendix `\ref{full-results-table}`{=latex}); scaffold-step dimensions carry 90 of the 100 raw points, and a leaderboard on those seven dimensions alone reproduces the full-rubric system ranking exactly on both *Falllösung* corpora (Spearman ρ = `{python} fmt(_rcore('benchathon'),2)`/`{python} fmt(_rcore('zjs'),2)`; `Table~\ref{tbl-reasoning-core}`{=latex}). The per-dimension scores in @fig-rubric-dim therefore track identifiable components of the reasoning process. Grundprinzipien, the short-form Doctrinal Principles corpus, uses a smaller 4-dimension rubric (result correctness, legal knowledge, subsumption, clarity). It is not mapped to the grade-point scale, since it does not follow the full *Gutachtenstil* scaffold.

The judge is blind to solution origin and receives inputs in a fixed (task, reference, candidate) order. The primary judge is GPT-5.4-mini^[Configuration in Appendix `\ref{llm-judge-configuration}`{=latex}, prompt in Appendix `\ref{evaluation-prompts-for-the-llm-judge}`{=latex}. The ARR-reviewed version used GPT-5-mini as primary judge; the final version re-scored every generation with GPT-5.4-mini and extended cross-validation to six judges – tier-level conclusions unchanged, absolute scores a calibrated ordering (Limitations).]. Judge-based evaluation may itself be model-dependent. We therefore cross-validate the judge against a multi-rater human-grading layer on the Benchathon subset. We also run two stability checks on that subset: *within-judge stochasticity* (k=3 re-runs of GPT-5.4-mini) and *between-judge bias* (additional evaluations by Opus-4.7, Gemini-3.1-Pro, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, and Sonnet-4.6). Both are reported in RQ5. Outputs conform to a predefined JSON schema. Non-conforming outputs are retried, and reported as evaluation failures if they cannot be parsed.


## Human evaluation {#sec-human-eval}
Human evaluation runs on a controlled subset of 45 Benchathon solutions: one traditional human, one human-AI co-created, and one LLM-generated solution per Benchathon task. Human submissions are the median-expertise participant per legal area (*Bereich*). LLM submissions are drawn through a tier-balanced rotation across closed-flagship, efficiency-oriented, and open-weight reference systems. Each selected solution receives three independent blind reviews and one author-informed creator review (180 reviews total). Reviewers grade against the same rubric as the LLM judge and form the IRR pool used to validate it. Creator reviews are excluded from IRR, since task authors have privileged knowledge. The selection procedure, the seven-grader roster, and the $m=5$ blind subset (the graders who performed blind reviews; each solution is reviewed by three of them) are described in `Appendix~\ref{full-benchathon-human-evaluation-assignment-procedure}`{=latex}.

We report descriptive agreement statistics – Spearman, Pearson, MAE, Cohen's $\kappa$ [@cohen1960coefficient], ICC(2,1) and ICC(2,k) [@shrout1979intraclass; @koo2016guideline] – alongside the Calderon alt-test [@calderon2025] in RQ5. Each answers a distinct question – rank vs. absolute agreement, error magnitude, the pass/fail threshold decision, and the reliability ceiling of the human pool itself (per-statistic rationale in Appendix `\ref{judge-human-agreement-statistics}`{=latex}). The Calderon alt-test is the primary criterion: it is purpose-built to decide whether the judge can stand in for a blind reviewer. For the alt-test we follow the blind-pool procedure at $\varepsilon=0.15$ (Calderon's skilled-annotator tier), with Benjamini-Yekutieli FDR at $q=0.05$ across the $m$ blind reviewers. We also report the single-expert variant against the un-blind creator grade at $\varepsilon=0.20$ (with $\varepsilon=0.15$ as a sensitivity check). Following Calderon's single-expert case, this variant treats the un-blind creator grade as the sole reference: each blind reviewer and the judge are scored by absolute distance to that single creator grade on every shared solution, and $\omega$ is the fraction of the $m$ blind reviewers the judge matches or beats. So it needs one creator grade per solution, not a pool of expert votes. The judge passes when the winning rate $\omega \geq 0.5$. The primary judge is GPT-5.4-mini; we additionally re-score the same picks with Opus-4.7, Gemini-3.1-Pro, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, and Sonnet-4.6 (per-judge alt-test in `Tables~\ref{tbl-calderon-per-annotator-human}`{=latex} and `\ref{tbl-calderon-per-annotator-llm}`{=latex}). The central question is not whether the LLM judge reproduces a single human grade. It is whether its deviation is comparable to that among human graders.

# Results

Unless otherwise noted, scores in this section are the LLM judge's rubric scores, as 0-100 raw points or 0-18 German grade points. The human-grading layer covers the 45-pick validation design: one author-informed creator review and three blind reviews per pick (180 reviews total). The released analysis pipeline regenerates every number reported below from the data files published with the paper.^[<https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20489788>]

<!-- Anchor the main leaderboard table* float here, at the top of Results, so
the wide float lands on the RQ1 page. The table body is built by the chunk
between RQ1 and RQ2, which writes assets/tbl_main_leaderboard.tex; \input is
resolved at LaTeX time, so the file exists regardless of chunk source order. -->
```{=latex}
\input{assets/tbl_main_leaderboard.tex}
```

## RQ1: Overall performance of LLM systems and humans

`Table~\ref{tbl-main-leaderboard}`{=latex} gives the unified per-system view across the three corpora; per-corpus full leaderboards with surface-metric columns are in `Appendix~\ref{full-results-table}`{=latex}. On Benchathon, the top of the table is a closed-flagship cluster – Opus-4.7, Gemini-3.1-Pro, and GPT-5.4 in the upper 60s on the 0-100 judge scale, with pass rates above 90% – followed by an efficiency band in the upper 50s to lower 60s. The strongest open-weight system, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, trails the top of the flagship cluster by roughly `{python} fmt(b('claude-opus-4-7') - b('deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Pro'),0)` raw points. The open-weight tier spans a wide range; the nominal flagship Qwen3-235B sits `{python} fmt(b('claude-opus-4-7') - b('Qwen/Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507'),0)` raw points behind the lead and underperforms its size here, despite no truncation. The same ordering broadly reproduces on ZJS, where GPT-5.4 leads and the weakest open-weight system, Llama-4, trails the field; per-system scores stay in `Table~\ref{tbl-main-leaderboard}`{=latex}.

Within-task variance is substantial. Under the GPT-5.4-mini judge, per-system standard deviations across the Benchathon tasks span roughly 8-17 raw points across all tiers. So single-task performance is a noisy signal, even for top-tier systems. On Benchathon, the human cohort scores in the mid-range of the LLM distribution under both conditions, shifted upward under co-creation (see human rows in `Table~\ref{tbl-main-leaderboard}`{=latex} and @fig-human-vs-ai in RQ4).

The same flagship cluster leads on the short-form Doctrinal Principles corpus, with Sonnet-4.6 joining it. Doctrinal Principles items carry a Ja/Nein (yes/no) decision label; across systems, decision accuracy ranges from `{python} fmtpct(min((r['metrics'].get('accuracy') or 0) for r in grundprinzipien_summary), 0)` to `{python} fmtpct(max((r['metrics'].get('accuracy') or 0) for r in grundprinzipien_summary), 0)`.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
# Table helpers (_tex_escape, emit_tex_table) and a few formatting maps used
# by both this body chunk and downstream appendix chunks. Defined here so the
# unified main leaderboard chunk below - which sits inside the Results
# section to anchor the table* float near RQ1/RQ2 - can use them without
# depending on the deferred chunks at the end of the document.
def _fmt(v):
    if v is None: return "-"
    if isinstance(v, float):
        return f"{v:.2g}" if abs(v) < 1 else f"{v:.0f}"
    return str(v)

_tier_label = {"flagship": "Flagship", "efficiency": "Efficiency",
               "open_reference": "Open ref."}
_access_label = {"provider_api": "Provider API",
                 "managed_inference": "Managed inf."}


def _tex_escape(s):
    """Minimal LaTeX escape for cells with text from the dataset."""
    if s is None:
        return "---"
    s = str(s)
    replacements = (
        ("\\", r"\textbackslash{}"),
        ("&", r"\&"), ("%", r"\%"), ("$", r"\$"), ("#", r"\#"),
        ("_", r"\_"), ("{", r"\{"), ("}", r"\}"),
        ("~", r"\textasciitilde{}"), ("^", r"\textasciicircum{}"),
        ("—", "---"), ("–", "--"),
    )
    for a, b in replacements:
        s = s.replace(a, b)
    return s


def emit_tex_table(label, caption, headers, rows, *,
                   col_spec=None, span_two_columns=False,
                   first_col_left=True, tex_escape=True,
                   tabcolsep_pt=4, fontsize_cmd=r"\footnotesize",
                   placement="t", fill_width=False, shrink_to_fit=False):
    """Print a native LaTeX float wrapping a booktabs tabular.

    fill_width=True switches the inner environment from ``tabular`` to
    ``tabular*`` with ``@{\\extracolsep{\\fill}}`` so the table stretches to
    the full text-column width of the float (\\columnwidth / \\textwidth)
    without rescaling fonts.

    shrink_to_fit=True wraps the tabular in ``\\resizebox{<width>}{!}{...}``
    so over-wide tables shrink to the float width instead of bleeding into
    the right margin. Forces a plain ``tabular`` (not ``tabular*``) because
    ``\\resizebox`` can't take an explicit-width tabular* as its argument.
    Use this for appendix tables with many numeric columns.
    """
    n_cols = len(headers)
    if col_spec is None:
        col_spec = ("l" if first_col_left else "c") + "c" * (n_cols - 1)
    env = "table*" if span_two_columns else "table"
    target_w = r"\textwidth" if span_two_columns else r"\columnwidth"
    out = [
        f"\\begin{{{env}}}[{placement}]",
        r"\centering" + fontsize_cmd,
        r"\caption{" + caption + "}",
        f"\\label{{{label}}}",
        f"\\setlength{{\\tabcolsep}}{{{tabcolsep_pt}pt}}",
    ]
    if shrink_to_fit:
        out.append(f"\\resizebox{{{target_w}}}{{!}}{{%")
        out.append(f"\\begin{{tabular}}{{{col_spec}}}")
    elif fill_width:
        col_spec_filled = "@{\\extracolsep{\\fill}}" + col_spec + "@{}"
        out.append(f"\\begin{{tabular*}}{{{target_w}}}{{{col_spec_filled}}}")
    else:
        out.append(f"\\begin{{tabular}}{{{col_spec}}}")
    out += [
        r"\toprule",
        " & ".join(headers) + r" \\",
        r"\midrule",
    ]
    for r in rows:
        if len(r) == 1:
            out.append(r[0] + r" \\")
            continue
        cells = [(_tex_escape(c) if tex_escape else (c if c is not None else "---"))
                 for c in r]
        out.append(" & ".join(cells) + r" \\")
    out.append(r"\bottomrule")
    if shrink_to_fit:
        out.append(r"\end{tabular}")
        out.append(r"}%  end \resizebox")
    else:
        out.append(r"\end{tabular*}" if fill_width else r"\end{tabular}")
    out.append(f"\\end{{{env}}}")
    out.append("")
    # Write the LaTeX to a per-label asset file and emit `\input{}` via a
    # raw-LaTeX block. Without this, Pandoc parses any `\begin{table*}` in
    # the chunk's `output: asis` stream as a Pandoc-managed float and its
    # caption brace counter truncates `\caption{...}` arguments that contain
    # nested math braces (e.g. `$\bar{\Delta}_{\text{dir}}$` - the inner
    # `}` is read as the close of `\caption{`). Routing through `\input{}`
    # bypasses Pandoc's float / caption processing entirely.
    from pathlib import Path as _Path
    _assets = _Path("assets")
    _assets.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    _safe = label.replace("-", "_")
    _path = _assets / f"{_safe}.tex"
    _path.write_text("\n".join(out), encoding="utf-8")
    print("```{=latex}")
    print(f"\\input{{assets/{_safe}.tex}}")
    print("```")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

# Unified main leaderboard across the three corpora. Sits in the Results
# section (between RQ1 and RQ2) so the table* float lands near its first
# reference. Replaces the previously separate per-corpus body tables; the
# surface-metric-rich per-corpus leaderboards live in Appendix~E. The two
# Benchathon human-baseline rows are pinned at the bottom.

import math, statistics
from collections import defaultdict

# Raw cells in the table are rendered as "mean ± half-CI" where half-CI is the
# half-width of a 95% interval on the per-system mean. Two derivations:
#   - Benchathon: percentile bootstrap CI from per-generation raw scores in
#     model_evals (scipy.stats.bootstrap).
#   - ZJS / Grundprinzipien: t-based CI from the per-system stdev added to the
#     summary JSONs by derive_zjs_summary.py and derive_grundprinzipien_summary.py
#     (Welford's online algorithm).

import numpy as np
from scipy import stats as _sp_stats

_BENCH_BOOT_RNG = np.random.default_rng(7)

def _bench_boot_ci(raws, n_boot=2000):
    """Half-width of a 95% percentile-bootstrap CI on the mean."""
    if len(raws) < 3:
        return None
    res = _sp_stats.bootstrap(
        (np.asarray(raws, dtype=float),), np.mean,
        n_resamples=n_boot, method="percentile",
        random_state=_BENCH_BOOT_RNG,
    )
    return (res.confidence_interval.high - res.confidence_interval.low) / 2.0

def _analytic_ci_half(mean, stdev, n):
    """Half-width of a 95% Gaussian CI on the mean (1.96 · SE)."""
    if mean is None or stdev is None or n is None or n <= 1:
        return None
    lo, hi = _sp_stats.norm.interval(0.95, loc=mean, scale=stdev / math.sqrt(n))
    return (hi - lo) / 2.0

_bench_by_sys_full = defaultdict(list)
for r in (model_evals or []):
    _bench_by_sys_full[r["system"]].append(r)

def _bench_agg(sys_id):
    rs = _bench_by_sys_full.get(sys_id) or []
    raws = [x["raw_score"] for x in rs if x.get("raw_score") is not None]
    ps   = [bool(x["passed"]) for x in rs if x.get("passed") is not None]
    return (len(raws) if raws else None,
            statistics.mean(raws) if raws else None,
            _bench_boot_ci(raws),
            (sum(ps) / len(ps)) if ps else None)

_zjs_by_sys_full = {r["system"]: r for r in (zjs_summary or [])}
def _zjs_agg(sys_id):
    r = _zjs_by_sys_full.get(sys_id)
    if not r: return (None, None, None, None)
    m = r.get("metrics") or {}
    mean = m.get("llm_judge_falloesung_raw_mean")
    stdev = m.get("llm_judge_falloesung_raw_stdev")
    n_w = m.get("llm_judge_falloesung_raw_n") or r.get("n_generations")
    return (r.get("n_generations"), mean,
            _analytic_ci_half(mean, stdev, n_w),
            m.get("pass_rate"))

_gp_by_sys_full = {r["system"]: r for r in (grundprinzipien_summary or [])}
def _gp_agg(sys_id):
    r = _gp_by_sys_full.get(sys_id)
    if not r: return (None, None, None, None, None)
    m = r.get("metrics") or {}
    mean = m.get("judge_raw_mean")
    stdev = m.get("judge_raw_stdev")
    n_w = m.get("judge_raw_n") or r.get("n_generations")
    return (r.get("n_generations"), mean,
            _analytic_ci_half(mean, stdev, n_w),
            m.get("judge_pass_rate"),
            m.get("accuracy"))

_all_sys_ids = set(_bench_by_sys_full) | set(_zjs_by_sys_full) | set(_gp_by_sys_full)

def _mean_avail(sys_id):
    vals = []
    for fn in (_bench_agg, _zjs_agg, _gp_agg):
        v = fn(sys_id)
        if v[1] is not None: vals.append(v[1])
    return statistics.mean(vals) if vals else 0.0

_sys_order = sorted(_all_sys_ids, key=lambda s: -_mean_avail(s))

def _raw_ci(mean, half):
    """Compact 'mean ± half' cell; falls back to '---' if mean missing."""
    if mean is None:
        return "---"
    if half is None:
        return f"{mean:.1f}"
    return f"{mean:.1f}\\,$\\pm$\\,{half:.1f}"

def _pct(v):  return "---" if v is None else f"{100*v:.0f}\\%"
def _n(v):    return "---" if v is None else str(v)

_rows = []
for sys_id in _sys_order:
    bn, br, br_ci, bp = _bench_agg(sys_id)
    zn, zr, zr_ci, zp = _zjs_agg(sys_id)
    gn, gr_, gr_ci, gp_r, ga = _gp_agg(sys_id)
    _rows.append([
        _tex_escape(alias_for(sys_id)),
        _n(bn), _raw_ci(br, br_ci), _pct(bp),
        _n(zn), _raw_ci(zr, zr_ci), _pct(zp),
        _n(gn), _raw_ci(gr_, gr_ci), _pct(gp_r), _pct(ga),
    ])

def _human_row(label, raws, passed):
    if not raws:
        return [label, "0", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---"]
    return [label, str(len(raws)),
            _raw_ci(statistics.mean(raws), _bench_boot_ci(raws)),
            f"{100*sum(passed)/len(passed):.0f}\\%",
            "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---"]

_human_all_raw  = _human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][0] + _human_judge_by_variant["ai"][0]
_human_all_pass = _human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][1] + _human_judge_by_variant["ai"][1]

_lines = [
    r"\begin{table*}[!t]",
    r"\centering\footnotesize",
    "\\caption{Main leaderboard across the three corpora. Per-system mean "
    "LLM-judge raw score (0--100, mean\\,$\\pm$\\,half-width of a 95\\% CI: "
    "percentile bootstrap $B=2000$ on Benchathon for small per-system $n$, "
    "analytic 1.96\\,$\\cdot$\\,SE on ZJS and Doctrinal Principles) and pass "
    "rate. Doctrinal Principles also reports Ja/Nein (yes/no) decision accuracy. "
    "Systems are ranked by the mean of their available per-corpus raw scores. "
    "The two human-baseline rows use the LLM judge's grades of human-written "
    "Benchathon solutions.}",
    r"\label{tbl-main-leaderboard}",
    r"\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}",
    r"\begin{tabular*}{\textwidth}{@{\extracolsep{\fill}} l rrr rrr rrrr @{}}",
    r"\toprule",
    r" & \multicolumn{3}{c}{Benchathon}"
    r" & \multicolumn{3}{c}{ZJS}"
    r" & \multicolumn{4}{c}{Doctrinal Principles} \\",
    r"\cmidrule(lr){2-4} \cmidrule(lr){5-7} \cmidrule(lr){8-11}",
    r"System / Group & $n$ & Raw & Pass"
    r" & $n$ & Raw & Pass"
    r" & $n$ & Raw & Pass & Acc. \\",
    r"\midrule",
]
for row in _rows:
    _lines.append(" & ".join(row) + r" \\")

_lines.append(r"\midrule")
for label, raws, passed in [
    ("Human (all)",       _human_all_raw, _human_all_pass),
    (r"\quad traditional", _human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][0], _human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][1]),
    (r"\quad co-creation", _human_judge_by_variant["ai"][0],    _human_judge_by_variant["ai"][1]),
]:
    _lines.append(" & ".join(_human_row(label, raws, passed)) + r" \\")

_lines += [r"\bottomrule", r"\end{tabular*}", r"\end{table*}", ""]
# Write to an asset file and \input it from an earlier anchor (right after the
# Results-section intro) so this wide table* float is born early enough to land
# on the RQ1 page rather than drifting down onto the RQ4 figure's page.
from pathlib import Path as _Path
_Path("assets").mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
_ = (_Path("assets") / "tbl_main_leaderboard.tex").write_text("\n".join(_lines), encoding="utf-8")  # assign so write_text's char-count return value isn't emitted as output
```

## RQ2: Scaling and system effects

Closed flagship systems from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google occupy the top of every per-corpus leaderboard. Efficiency-tier closed systems and the strongest open-weight system (DeepSeek-V4-Pro) form an overlapping mid-band. Closed-API dominance narrows below the flagship tier. By tier, the flagship-vs-open-weight gap is `{python} f"{(_ta('benchathon','by_tier','flagship','mean_raw') or 0) - (_ta('benchathon','by_tier','open_reference','mean_raw') or 0):.0f}"` raw points on Benchathon and narrows to `{python} f"{(_ta('zjs','by_tier','flagship','mean_raw') or 0) - (_ta('zjs','by_tier','open_reference','mean_raw') or 0):.0f}"` on ZJS. The closed-vs-open-weights gap shrinks similarly, from `{python} f"{(_ta('benchathon','by_weights','closed','mean_raw') or 0) - (_ta('benchathon','by_weights','open','mean_raw') or 0):.0f}"` to `{python} f"{(_ta('zjs','by_weights','closed','mean_raw') or 0) - (_ta('zjs','by_weights','open','mean_raw') or 0):.0f}"` raw points. The same direction holds on Doctrinal Principles. The flagship-vs-open-weight ordering therefore survives all three task formats and the 4-dimension rubric variant. We frame these as system-level effects, not model-level ones. Black-box API access entangles the open-closed split with provider-side prompts, decoding, post-processing, and possible retrieval or tool use (see Limitations).

## RQ3: Evaluation validity - automatic metric correlation

Across all three corpora, lexical surface metrics correlate with the judge's rubric raw score more strongly than embedding-based metrics do. This runs partly against the common expectation that semantic-embedding metrics dominate lexical ones on long-form legal text. On Benchathon the lexical metrics lead: METEOR (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('meteor',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`), ROUGE (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('rouge',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`), and BLEU (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('bleu',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`). MoverScore (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('moverscore',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`) is the one embedding metric on par with them. BERTScore (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('bertscore',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`) and sentence-embedding similarity (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('semantic_similarity',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`) trail. The same ordering broadly holds on ZJS and Doctrinal Principles (`Appendix~\ref{automatic-metric-vs.-llm-judge-correlation}`{=latex}: `Table~\ref{tbl-metric-correlation}`{=latex}, @fig-metric-correlation). This fits the highly templated structure of doctrinal German exam writing, where reference and high-quality system answers share substantial n-gram material around statutory citations and the *Obersatz-Definition-Subsumtion-Ergebnis* scaffolding.

At the per-system level, the ordering shifts. Embedding-based metrics rank systems at least as well as lexical ones. On Benchathon, ROUGE leads (Spearman `{python} fmtp(_rc('benchathon','rouge'))`), but BERTScore (`{python} fmtp(_rc('benchathon','bertscore'))`) and MoverScore (`{python} fmtp(_rc('benchathon','moverscore'))`) outrank BLEU (`{python} fmtp(_rc('benchathon','bleu'))`) and METEOR (`{python} fmtp(_rc('benchathon','meteor'))`). On ZJS, BERTScore is the single strongest ranker (`{python} fmtp(_rc('zjs','bertscore'))`). A leaderboard built on a strong surface or embedding metric would broadly preserve the judge's coarse-tier ordering, but disagree on the mid-tier. This motivates a calibrated LLM judge as the primary scoring instrument.

A natural concern is that the lexical advantage could reflect a judge-specific preference for reference-like text rather than a property of the task. Two analyses on the 45-pick validation subset test this against the mean blind-human grade and say otherwise: the lexical correlations persist under human anchoring (r≈`{python} fmtp(_mvh('meteor'))` pooled), and the residual diagnostic – correlating (judge − mean blind grade) with each lexical metric – comes out *negative* for all three: relative to blind human graders, the judge *under*-rewards high-overlap solutions, the opposite sign of a reference-style bias (`Table~\ref{tbl-metric-vs-human}`{=latex} in Appendix `\ref{automatic-metric-vs.-llm-judge-correlation}`{=latex}, with per-metric values, length-controlled partials, and small-$n$ caveats).

## RQ4: Human-AI co-creation under LLM-judge scoring

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-human-vs-ai
#| fig-cap: "Mean LLM-judge raw score (0-100) per group on the Benchathon validation subset, with 95% bootstrap CIs on each group's mean. The two AI bars pool per-generation scores across all evaluated LLM systems (*pooled*) and across the closed-flagship tier only (*flagship*)."
#| fig-pos: 't'

# Single-column figure (Quarto-native crossref via @fig-human-vs-ai). Compact
# four-group bar chart sized for a single column of the ACL two-column
# layout (~3.3 in wide). The flagship-only AI bar makes the co-creation ≈
# closed-flagship-tier comparison visually immediate.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats as _sp_stats

_HVSA_BOOT_RNG = np.random.default_rng(11)

def _hvsa_boot_ci(xs, n=2000):
    arr = np.asarray([float(x) for x in xs if x is not None], dtype=float)
    if arr.size < 3:
        return (float(arr.mean()) if arr.size else 0.0, 0.0, 0.0)
    res = _sp_stats.bootstrap(
        (arr,), np.mean, n_resamples=n, method="percentile",
        random_state=_HVSA_BOOT_RNG,
    )
    return (float(arr.mean()),
            float(res.confidence_interval.low),
            float(res.confidence_interval.high))

_flagship_ids = {s["model_id"] for s in (systems_meta or []) if s.get("tier") == "flagship"}
_flagship_raws = [r["raw_score"] for r in (model_evals or [])
                  if r.get("raw_score") is not None and r.get("system") in _flagship_ids]

_groups = [
    ("Human\ntraditional", _human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][0]),
    ("Human\nco-creation", _human_judge_by_variant["ai"][0]),
]
if model_evals:
    _ai_raws = [r["raw_score"] for r in model_evals if r["raw_score"] is not None]
    _groups.append(("AI all\n(pooled)", _ai_raws))
if _flagship_raws:
    _groups.append(("AI flagship\n(closed)", _flagship_raws))

_labels = [g[0] for g in _groups]
_points, _los, _his = zip(*(_hvsa_boot_ci(g[1]) for g in _groups))
_ns = [len(g[1]) for g in _groups]

_fig, _ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(2.95, 1.9))
_xs = range(len(_groups))
_ax.bar(_xs, _points,
        yerr=[[p - l for p, l in zip(_points, _los)],
              [h - p for p, h in zip(_points, _his)]],
        color=["#6c8eb0", "#b08056", "#a89bc8", "#6f5fa0"][:len(_groups)],
        capsize=2.5, edgecolor="black", linewidth=0.4, width=0.55)
for _x, _p, _n in zip(_xs, _points, _ns):
    _ax.text(_x, _p + 1.5, f"{_p:.1f}", ha="center", va="bottom",
             fontsize=6.5, weight="bold")
    _ax.text(_x, 2, f"$n$={_n}", ha="center", va="bottom",
             fontsize=5.8, color="#444")
_ax.set_xticks(list(_xs))
_ax.set_xticklabels(_labels, fontsize=6.5)
_ax.set_ylabel("LLM-judge raw (0-100)", fontsize=6.5)
_ax.tick_params(axis="y", labelsize=6)
_ax.set_ylim(0, 100)
_ax.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)
_ax.yaxis.grid(True, linestyle=":", linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.6)
_ax.set_axisbelow(True)
plt.tight_layout(pad=0.2)
plt.show()
```

Co-creation solutions score higher than traditional ones on Benchathon – by `{python} fmtp(r4.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad'),1)` raw points on average, about `{python} fmtp((r4.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad') or 0)*18/100,1)` grade points on the 0-18 scale (@fig-human-vs-ai; participant-clustered 95% CI [`{python} fmtp(r4.get('ci95_lo'),1)`, `{python} fmtp(r4.get('ci95_hi'),1)`]). The gap holds within the `{python} f"{r4.get('n_participants_paired')}"` participants who completed both arms^[Participants who submitted both traditional and co-creation solutions.]. It repeats across all three legal domains. It is in fact a lower bound on the per-participant gain: less-experienced participants skipped traditional items more often than co-creation ones, leaving the traditional pool expertise-biased upward (full robustness arc in `Appendix~\ref{rq4-supplementary}`{=latex}). With that lift, the AI-assisted humans sit alongside the best standalone LLMs we evaluated. A TOST equivalence test rules out any difference of more than $\pm 5$ raw points against the closed-flagship tier (`Table~\ref{tbl-main-leaderboard}`{=latex}, `Appendix~\ref{rq4-supplementary}`{=latex}). Re-graded by blind human reviewers on the RQ5 validation subset, the gap shrinks to about `{python} f"{round(abs((r4h.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad') or 0)))}"` raw points. That is `{python} fmtp(abs((r4.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad') or 0) - (r4h.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad') or 0)),0)` points smaller than the LLM-judge estimate, a small absolute discrepancy. It is consistent with the GPT-5.4-mini judge's near-zero content-dependent calibration offset, and within the blind-pool's inter-rater spread (Limitations).

## RQ5: Grading reliability

The reliability question is simple: can the LLM judge stand in for a blind human reviewer? We answer it in three stages: how closely the judge agrees with humans per solution, whether it clears the formal substitution bar, and what its disagreements look like when they happen.


Across the validation subset, the primary GPT-5.4-mini judge agrees with the per-solution mean of the blind reviewers at Pearson r=`{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('pearson_raw'))`, pass/fail Cohen's $\kappa$=`{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('passfail_cohen_kappa'))`, and MAE `{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('mae_raw'),1)` raw / `{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('mae_grade_points'),1)` grade points. The per-solution offset is just `{python} fmtp(r5_jh.get('judge_minus_pool_mean_raw'),2)` raw points. The reviewers themselves disagree by an average within-annotation max-min spread of `{python} fmt(r5_irr.get('mean_within_ann_spread_raw'),1)` raw points, comparable to the several-grade-point range Hufeld [@hufeld2024] documents for German legal grading. The pool reaches ICC(2,$k$)=`{python} fmt(r5_irr.get('icc_2_k_raw'))`. So the judge sits inside the cloud of human disagreement, not outside it (`Table~\ref{tbl-agreement}`{=latex}).


Per-solution agreement does not by itself show that the judge can *replace* a randomly drawn reviewer. The Calderon alt-test sets a stricter bar: $\varepsilon=0.15$ for the blind grader pool (*skilled*) and $\varepsilon=0.2$ for the single un-blind creator reference (*expert*), with Benjamini-Yekutieli FDR (`§\ref{sec-human-eval}`{=latex}). We apply it across six judges: the primary GPT-5.4-mini, plus Opus-4.7, Gemini-3.1-Pro, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, and Sonnet-4.6.


For the blind pool, two judges clear the $\omega \geq 0.5$ replacement bar on the human-authored picks: GPT-5.4-mini at $\omega=$ `{python} fmt(_cpj('baseline_human').get('winning_rate'),2)` and Opus-4.7 at $\omega=$ `{python} fmt(_cpj('cfgB_opus_human').get('winning_rate'),2)`. Sonnet-4.6 ($\omega=$ `{python} fmt(_cpj('sonnet_human').get('winning_rate'),2)`) and Gemini-3.1-Pro ($\omega=$ `{python} fmt(_cpj('cfgB_gemini_human').get('winning_rate'),2)`) fall one annotator-rejection short. DeepSeek-V4-Pro and Qwen3.5-397B-A17B fail at $\omega \leq 0.20$. At $m=5$ these are point estimates; we read the two-judge pass as directional, not established (Limitations).
